front: Poem: Clarissa's 'two-edged weapon' cuts Belinda's hair
back: The Rape of the Lock (Alexander Pope)

front: Poem: guardian sylph sliced in half
back: The Rape of the Lock (Alexander Pope)

front: Poem: Cave of Spleen — gnome Umbriel given vial of 'fainting fears' and bag of 'Force of Female Lungs'; Queen served by 'Ill-Nature' and 'Affection'
back: The Rape of the Lock (Alexander Pope)

front: Poem: lock traced through seal rings, belt buckle, baby's whistle
back: The Rape of the Lock (Alexander Pope)

front: Poem: set in stars to 'add new glory to the shining sphere'
back: The Rape of the Lock (Alexander Pope)

front: Poem: goddess Dulness crowns Lewis Theobald or Colley Cibber
Poem: Edmund Curll in pissing contest; slips in excrement, saved by goddess Cloacina
Poem: 'A little learning is a dang'rous thing' / 'Drink deep, or taste not the Pierian Spring'
Poem: 'First follow NATURE' / 'Nature and Homer were, he found, the same'
Poem: Great Chain of Being — 'All are but parts of one stupendous whole, whose body Nature is, and God the soul'
Poem: 'Whatever is, is right'
Poem: man on 'isthmus of a middle state'
Poem: Voltaire called it 'the most sublime didactic poem ever'
Poem: Nabokov's Pale Fire references Zembla from it
Poem: 'damn with faint praise'; 'Who breaks a butterfly upon a wheel?' (about Sporus/John Hervey)
Poem: compares bad writer to spider — 'he spins the slight, self-pleasing thread anew'
Poem: opens 'Shut, shut the door, good John!'
Poem: coined 'Eternal sunshine of the spotless mind'
Poem: 'The world forgetting by the world forgot'
Poem: heartbreak over Lady Montagu; later attacked her as 'Sappho'
Author: Iliad translation opens 'Achilles' wrath, to Greece the direful spring'
Author: Johnson: 'no age or nation can pretend to equal'
Author: 'Peri Bathous, or the Art of Sinking in Poetry' — parody of Longinus's On the Sublime
Author: Newton's epitaph — 'Nature and Nature's laws lay hid in night: God said, Let Newton be! and all was light'
Author: poisoned bookseller Edmund Curll as practical joke
Author: walked London with Great Dane and two loaded pistols
Poem: fifty sylphs guard petticoat; Ariel guards spaniel Shock; warns Belinda in dream
back: The Rape of the Lock (Alexander Pope)

front: Poem: bodies 'chang'd to various Forms' — living teapots, maids turn'd bottles, a solitary pipkin
back: The Rape of the Lock (Alexander Pope)

front: Poem: Baron builds altar of billet-doux; uses 'forfex' after game of ombre at Hampton Court
back: The Rape of the Lock (Alexander Pope)

front: Poem: dedication names Le Comte de Gabalis as source for the supernatural 'machinery'
back: The Rape of the Lock (Alexander Pope)

front: Poem: Clarissa's speech 'charms strike the sight, but merit wins the soul' — parodies Sarpedon to Glaucus
back: The Rape of the Lock (Alexander Pope)

front: Poem: Theobald's three-book version crowns him successor to Elkanah Settle; Cibber called 'Bays' in four-book version
Poem: inspired by Dryden's Mac Flecknoe (Thomas Shadwell as dull hero)
Poem: 'words are like leaves; and where they most abound, much fruit of sense beneath is rarely found'
Poem: 'a needless Alexandrine ends the song' — self-referential line about meter
Poem: 'Hope springs eternal in the human breast'
Poem: 'The proper study of mankind is man' / 'Know then thyself, presume not God to scan'
Poem: 'poor Indian' whose 'faithful dog' guides to heaven — noble savage example
Poem: preternatural senses — would 'die of a rose in aromatic pain'
Poem: Bacon called 'wisest, brightest, meanest of mankind'
Poem: Addison satirized as 'Atticus' — 'blessed with each talent' but 'too fond to rule alone / Bear, like the Turk, no brother near the throne'
Poem: Hervey as Sporus called 'mere white curd of ass's milk'
Poem: thanks Arbuthnot for helping 'through this long disease, my life'
Author: 'Windsor Forest' — condemns ravages of Normans, composed for Peace of Utrecht
Poem: source of Forster's title Where Angels Fear to Tread
